1: Three years of famine; David seeks YHWH’s face; YHWH says it is for Saul’s violation of the Gibeonites. The famine traced to a covenantal violation from the previous generation is the type of the inherited consequences of sin. Paul’s argument in Romans 5:12: “sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin” — the whole human family inherits the consequences of Adam’s covenant violation. David seeking YHWH’s face to understand the source of the affliction is the type of the faithful who go to YHWH to diagnose the root of suffering rather than treating symptoms only.
2: The Gibeonites were not Israelites but a remnant of the Amorites — protected by the oath of Joshua (Josh 9). Israel had sworn to preserve them; Saul violated that oath. The binding force of a covenant oath — even sworn to non-Israelites, even binding on the next generation — is the type of the eternal covenant’s binding force. Christ’s new covenant is sealed with his own blood (Luke 22:20); its obligations bind all who enter it across every generation. The covenant oath sworn to the Gibeonites, binding on Israel long after Joshua’s death, is the type of the covenant oath that binds the church to Christ’s terms across all generations.
3: “What shall I do for you? How shall I make atonement (ʾăkappēr)?” David’s question about atonement for covenant violation is the OT’s central question about sin: how is covenant violation repaired? The answer in 2 Samuel 21:3-9 — through substitutionary death — is the type of the gospel’s answer. Christ’s death is the ultimate kipper (atonement): “he is the propitiation for our sins” (1 John 2:2). The king asking “how shall I make atonement?” is every human being before the covenant God; Christ’s cross is the divine answer to the question David can only type.
4: The Gibeonites: “It is not a matter of silver or gold between us and Saul, and we have no right to put anyone in Israel to death.” The refusal of monetary compensation for blood-guilt — the declaration that some violations cannot be settled by financial payment — is the type of the gospel’s declaration about sin: “you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold” (1 Peter 1:18). The atonement for blood-guilt requires the appropriate payment; the Gibeonites’ refusal of silver points toward the costly blood-price that alone can satisfy.
5: “The man who consumed us and who planned to destroy us.” The Gibeonite description of Saul’s genocidal intent is the type of the enemy’s program against the covenant people — and against humanity as a whole. John 10:10: “the thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy.” The one who planned to destroy the covenant-protected is the type of the enemy whose plan is annihilation. Christ comes as the answer to the destroying plan: “I came that they may have life and have it abundantly” — the contrast between the destroyer’s program and the anointed’s purpose.
6: “Let seven of his male descendants be handed over to us, to hang them before YHWH at Gibeah of Saul.” Seven sons as substitute for the covenant-violating king is the substitutionary principle: the sins of the fathers visited on the children (Exodus 20:5). The seven men who die for Saul’s violation of the Gibeonite covenant are types of the substitutionary death that clears the blood-guilt. But the NT’s reversal is decisive: “the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Matthew 20:28) — in Christ, the innocent dies in the place of the guilty, not the descendants for their ancestor.
7: The king spared Mephibosheth son of Jonathan because of the covenant oath between David and Jonathan. The covenant oath that shelters the one who would otherwise perish is the type of Christ’s covenant that shelters believers from the judgment that their guilt deserves. “There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1) — those in covenant with the anointed are sheltered by the oath sworn between the anointed and the Father. Mephibosheth is not spared by his own merit but by the covenant oath that preceded his existence; believers are saved by the covenant made before the foundation of the world (Ephesians 1:4).
8: The king took the two sons of Rizpah and the five sons of Michal and handed them over to the Gibeonites. The seven men handed over — innocent descendants of the covenant violator — are the type of those who bear punishment for inherited sin. But the NT transformation is complete: Christ bears the punishment for Adam’s descendants — “as one trespass led to condemnation for all men, so one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all men” (Romans 5:18). The seven bear the sin of one king; Christ bears the sin of all humanity. The substitution runs in the opposite direction: not the innocent many for the guilty one, but the innocent One for the guilty many.
9: The seven were hanged on the mountain before YHWH — all seven fell together in the first days of harvest. The execution before YHWH on the mountain — a public, covenant-sanctioned death that addresses the blood-guilt — is the type of the cross as a public execution before YHWH that addresses all humanity’s blood-guilt. Deuteronomy 21:23: “cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree” — Paul quotes this in Galatians 3:13: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, ‘cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree.’” The seven hanged before YHWH on the mountain are types of the one who hangs on the tree to bear the covenant curse.
10: Rizpah spread sackcloth on a rock and kept watch from the beginning of harvest until the rains fell, guarding the bodies from birds and beasts by day and night. The devoted vigil of the woman who would not let the executed bodies be dishonored is one of the most powerful images in Samuel. The women at the cross who “watched from afar” (Matthew 27:55-56; Mark 15:40; Luke 23:49) and the women at the tomb who came to anoint Christ’s body (Luke 24:1) are the NT instances of Rizpah’s faithfulness. The covenant devotion that refuses to abandon the crucified body — staying through the long watches — is the type of the women who honor the dead anointed.
11: David was told what Rizpah had done. The faithful woman’s vigil reaches the king’s ears — her covenant devotion generates a royal response. The faithfulness of those who honor the anointed’s fallen servants does not go unnoticed by the king. Matthew 26:13: the woman who anointed Jesus — “wherever this gospel is proclaimed in the whole world, what she has done will also be told in memory of her.” The anointed king hears of every act of covenant devotion performed in the shadow of his absence and will honor it at the resurrection.
12: David retrieves the bones of Saul and Jonathan from Jabesh-gilead — the men who had honored the fallen king at great risk (1 Samuel 31:11-13) are now honored in return by David’s action. The gathering of the honored dead is the type of the resurrection: “the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first” (1 Thessalonians 4:16). David gathers the scattered bones of the fallen anointed; Christ will gather the scattered bones of all his own at the resurrection.
13: The bones of Saul and Jonathan brought together with the bones of the seven executed sons. The gathering of all the honored dead — bringing scattered remains together — is the type of the general resurrection in which all who belong to the anointed will be gathered: “they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other” (Matthew 24:31). No bone is left behind; no servant of the covenant is forgotten. “Precious in the sight of YHWH is the death of his faithful ones” (Psalms 116:15) — the king who gathers the bones honors this truth.
14: They buried the bones in Zela of Benjamin, in the tomb of Kish, Saul’s father. “After this God was moved by prayer for the land.” The proper burial of the covenant dead — their return to the ancestral inheritance — and the divine response of relenting from the judgment is the type of the resurrection’s resolution of all outstanding accounts. The blood-guilt for the Gibeonites resolved; the bones of the fallen honored; YHWH’s face turned toward the land again — the full cycle of judgment, atonement, burial, and restoration is the type of the redemptive sequence culminating in the new creation.
15: War again between the Philistines and Israel — David went down and fought. The recurring warfare that follows every apparent settlement is the pattern of the anointed’s ongoing campaign against the enemies of the kingdom. “He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet” (1 Corinthians 15:25) — the last enemy to be destroyed is death (2 Samuel 21:26), but before that last enemy, there are many battles. The anointed who keeps returning to battle is the type of Christ’s unrelenting advance against the powers of the present age.
16: Ishbi-benob, a descendant of the giants, nearly killed David — his bronze spear weighed three hundred shekels, and he was girded with a new sword. The giant who nearly kills the anointed king is the type of the mortal threat that the powers of the old order pose to the anointed. Christ’s temptation in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11), Gethsemane’s agony (Luke 22:44), and the cross are the supreme moments of the giant’s near-victory against the anointed. The power of the enemy is real; it nearly destroys; only the intervention of another can save.
17: Abishai rescued David and killed the Philistine. David’s men swore: “You shall not go out to battle with us anymore, lest you extinguish the lamp of Israel.” The title “lamp of Israel” (nēr yiśrāʾēl) for the anointed king is the most theologically freighted title in the passage. Christ as “the light of the world” (John 8:12), the “true light that gives light to everyone” (John 1:9), is the fulfillment of the lamp-of-Israel metaphor. The fear that the anointed might be extinguished — that the source of Israel’s light might go dark — is the fear that the cross appeared to realize. The resurrection is the lamp that cannot be extinguished.
18: Another battle at Gob; Sibbecai the Hushathite killed Saph, another descendant of the giants. David’s servants defeating the giants that David himself can no longer face in battle is the type of the church continuing Christ’s victory against his enemies. Colossians 1:24: “I rejoice in my sufferings for your sake, and in my flesh I am filling up what is lacking in Christ’s afflictions for the sake of his body, that is, the church” — the church participates in completing the campaign that the anointed initiated. The servants fight the battles the anointed has commissioned them to fight.
19: Another battle at Gob; Elhanan killed Goliath the Gittite. The recurrence of Goliath-type enemies — giant after giant requiring defeat — is the type of the principalities and powers that Christ’s church confronts in every generation: “we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness” (Ephesians 6:12). Each generation of the anointed’s servants faces its own Goliath; each must be defeated by those the anointed has equipped and commissioned.
20: A man of great stature at Gath, with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot — a champion taunting Israel. The enemy who taunts the covenant people of the anointed is the type of the accuser who challenges the church’s legitimacy and standing. Revelation 12:10: “the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down, who accuses them day and night before our God.” The giant’s taunting (the same word used for Goliath’s challenge in 1 Sam 17) is the anti-anointed pattern: the enemy’s weapon is not only force but challenge and mockery of the covenant community’s claim to the anointed’s protection.
21: Jonathan son of Shimei, David’s brother, struck the giant down. The king’s own kinsman — a member of the royal family — killing the taunter is the type of Christ’s own work of silencing the accuser: “the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down” (Revelation 12:10) by the one who conquered through his own blood. The taunter who challenges the anointed’s people is answered not by the whole army but by the one who bears the anointed’s name and shares his family.
22: “These four were descendants of the giants in Gath, and they fell by the hand of David and his servants.” The concluding formula — four giants defeated by the anointed and his servants together — is the summary statement of the church’s mission: the battle against the enemies of the kingdom is a joint work of Christ and those who serve him. “We are more than conquerors through him who loved us” (Romans 8:37) — not apart from Christ, not by Christ alone without the participation of his servants, but together: “by the hand of David and his servants.” The anointed and his people share the victory over the giants of the old order.
